rules.description: Named const before every return. No call expression, ternary, or anonymous object on the return line itself.
Function and method names must not use banned verbs: handle, do, run, execute, perform. Exception: 'handle' is allowed only for DOM event handlers (e.g., handleClick, handleSubmit).
All boolean variables must carry a semantic prefix: is, has, can, should, did, needs, supports, allows.
No magic strings, template literals, or anonymous objects passed directly as assertion arguments. Extract into named consts first.

No computed expression (function call, method call, ternary) passed directly as an argument in a function call. The violation is at the argument position — not in variable assignment RHS, object property values, or array elements. Extract into a named const first. Applies to all callsites: assert, throw, console.log, and all function invocations.

Variable and parameter names must not be exactly a banned noun (standalone, not as part of a compound name). The full identifier must equal the banned word — compound names like `packageData`, `ruleObj`, `existingItems` are not violations. Banned nouns: data, info, obj, item, thing, res, req, ctx, cb, fn, val, tmp, arr.
A function should either orchestrate (call named functions) or implement (compute/transform) — not both. Functions that mix levels of abstraction violate the Single Level of Abstraction rule.
rules.exampleViolation: function processOrder(order) { validateOrder(order); const total = order.items.reduce((sum, i) => sum + i.price, 0); }
rules.exampleFix: function processOrder(order) { validateOrder(order); const total = computeOrderTotal(order); }

rules.description: Deeply nested conditionals (3+ levels) instead of guard clauses. Prefer early returns to reduce nesting.

Logically distinct groups of statements must be separated by exactly one blank line. Zero blank lines within a group. Never two or more consecutive blank lines. Groups are defined by semantic intent, not by statement type.
